claims.claim: Excited about stablecoins & onchain payments as crypto's clearest product-market fit, becoming a mainstream payments layer with regulatory/fintech tailwinds
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about AI x crypto: crypto supplying primitives for AI agents — programmable payments, verifiable identity/proof-of-personhood, provenance
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about scaling & infrastructure (L2s, zero-knowledge, account abstraction); ZK as a foundational primitive beyond scaling
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about DePIN — token incentives bootstrapping real-world networks (compute, wireless, energy, storage)
claims.label: absent

claims.claim: Concerned about memecoins & pure speculation crowding out durable building and degrading trust
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Concerned about low-float / high-FDV token structures distorting price discovery via unlock overhang
claims.label: absent

claims.claim: Risk-on / structurally constructive on the long-term building and adoption cycle (regulatory clarity, maturing infra, real usage), not a short-term price call
claims.label: confirmed

missed_changes: Onchain/financial PRIVACY became a dominant new thesis — repeatedly called lack of privacy a dealbreaker for institutions and championed privacy projects (Railgun, Aleo, Aztec, zkP2P, Seismic, etc.); not anticipated in the prediction

Built and heavily promoted a network-token vs. company-backed token taxonomy ('decentralized network marketplaces,' tokens as marketplace equity, 'arcade tokens' as the most underappreciated type, fee switches, fully-automatic buybacks)
Took a strong 'end of the foundation era' stance — advocating DUNAs/BORGs over offshore foundations as new legal structures for decentralized projects

New/sharpened concern that AI bots are degrading social networks to the point you can't tell humans apart, requiring sybil-resistant private ID (strong, specific endorsement of World/World ID as root of trust)
Surfaced cross-chain payment determinism (bridges not delivering exact amounts) as a concrete blocker to real onchain payments
Pushed a valuation/analytical view rejecting DCF and 'money' framing for L1 tokens, plus 'Bitcoin envy' criticism
Despite abstaining on tokens, expressed token-level views as framework illustrations: UNI/ETH/MKR as exemplary network tokens, FTT as a cautionary company-backed token, skepticism of BETH
Characterized acute scaling as largely 'solved' (questions shifted from gas to UX/wallets), a slight reframing of the scaling-urgency thesis even while still favoring more blockspace
notes: Window is Apr–Sep 2025. Four of seven prediction items are clearly confirmed at the stance level and recur repeatedly: payments/stablecoins (USDC, CCTP, x402, deterministic-output payment threads), AI x crypto (proof-of-human, identity-for-agents, trustable agent networks), ZK/infrastructure (Jolt zkVM, sumcheck/GKR/lookups, sequencer decentralization, Halmos), and the long-term risk-on/constructive regime (DC regulatory optimism, 'commercial viability is true north,' 'conditions are far better now'). Memecoin/speculation concern is strongly confirmed (memecoins have 'no inherent value,' 'just redistributing,' 'gambling products'). DePIN and low-float/high-FDV are absent — neither physical-infrastructure networks nor circulating-supply/unlock-overhang concerns appear in the provided statements (general network-bootstrapping is discussed but not applied to DePIN). No predicted stance was contradicted/reversed. The biggest gaps are omissions, not errors: the prediction missed how central PRIVACY and the token-taxonomy/legal-structure work would become.
